speaker: interviewer
text: This is Tape 5 of an interview with Mr. Aharon Golub. My name is Judith Ehrenfeld. Mr. Golub, you were telling me about your family, and what happened after the war. Could you back up just a little bit and tell me about how it is that you found your uncle? And-- or how he found you? While you were in the kibbutz in Israel, did you keep contact with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I knew that my uncle was in Canada. I don't remember how exactly we-- he knew I went to Israel. And I knew that he wasn't-- I knew the addresses of-- I knew the name of my relatives, my grandfather and my uncles.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I wrote them the letters, they told me that my uncle and my aunt that saved my life are now residing in Canada. And this is how we formed the contact. What else would you like to know? And how did you contact them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I wrote them a letter. In those days, we didn't use the telephone that freely. I wrote him a letter. And we were in touch since then. And he also urged me to come to the United States. And I always refused to come permanently. As a matter of fact, I didn't even want to come for a visit, because I felt I didn't have money to pay for the trip. But unfortunately, my grandfather died in between all that. And he left a small inheritance, which really didn't amount to much. But knowing that he left my father's share of a small building to his children. So since I now own some money, I agreed for them to advance me the money for a trip. And this is how I came to visit them. Then when I came here, they received me very warmly. These are relatives I've never known before. And they made me very comfortable. While I was here, I helped my uncle a little out. He had a store. So I helped him in the store. Because I always felt-- we were brought up in Israel that you have to pay for your upkeep, you know. So I felt always better doing that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then after that, after you met Ruth, you decided to stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When I met Ruth, she came from an unbelievable Zionistic home. Her father was so devoted to Israel and so is her sister and her mother. When I came to visit their home, I felt like I was back in Israel, all the Hebrew records and all the pictures. And her father was already a major contributor to UJA. In those days, when money was so valued, he didn't allow himself a lot of things for his family. But he gave thousands of dollars to the United Jewish Appeal.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was a very warm feeling belonging to the house. You know, as we were going out and we got to know each other, we decided to get married. And I remained in this country.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am very close with all my friends in Israel. We go to visit Israel regularly, every two years, every second year. And I am very much concerned about Israel's safety. And I am also contributing to the United Jewish Appeal, way above my means, as much as I feel I can afford. And this is a lot. And I'm also working for the United Jewish Appeal for the past 20 years as a volunteer. I solicit contributions from larger donors. And I've been doing it because I feel this is my obligation. I feel that the two greatest thing that happened in my life, besides my family and my children, is that I was fortunate enough to have to live in a period of the creation of the state of Israel. And the other major thing is to see the Russian Jewry being liberated. This, to me, I feel, was worth all the suffering, to live through and see those things happening in my lifetime. I consider myself very privileged that I lived during that period.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you think about your experience, how do you think that it was that you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's just luck. If there is a God, maybe it was meant to be. So it's difficult to-- I wasn't smarter than anybody else. It just happened to be this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it, do you think, that helped you to stay alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess basically it was the instinct of every human being, wanting to be alive and doing everything possible to stay alive. I did whatever I could. I had help from my family. My cousins, my uncle, my aunt, they have a great part in my survival. Without them, I could never make it through that special period when I was left behind in the forest. I'm a proud Jew. I'm happy to be Jewish. I think we're wonderful people. We don't bother anybody. We work. We earn a living. We are charitable. We help other people.

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel a great injustice was done to us. But what could you do about it? I think the creation of Israel is perhaps a compensation for all the suffering. Perhaps God works in mysterious ways. Maybe this was the price we had to pay for having a Jewish state. And your feet and your legs, how are they now?

speaker: interviewee
text: My feet are not in good condition. My left leg had to be amputated approximately 20 years ago. And I'm wearing a prosthetic. And my other foot is not in the best of condition. But I'm alive, I'm functioning. I ride a bike. I live a normal life. And I'm grateful for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you very much for the interview, Mr. Golub.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for having all the patience with me. This is a picture of my family in Poland, prior to the war. On the right is my father. Next to him is my mother. On the right side is my sister Chava. And to the left is myself, Aharon. And in the middle is my little sister Esther. This picture is from a group of prominent business people in the town of Ludwipol. The second from the left is my mother. On the bottom, the third, the two girls, is my older sister Chava and my younger sister Esther. And next to them, to the right, is myself sitting with my hands folded.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in, I would say, in 1936. It was a celebration of a party at the gentleman on the right, which was a partner with my father's business. This is a picture of my father, Baruch Golub and my older sister, Chavilah Golub. This picture was taken in my mother's studio of photography. It's a shame that the two of them died together at the same time. This is a picture, it was taken right after the war. From left to right is my cousin Shalom, my cousin Boris, his wife, my uncle Usher and my tante Chava and myself. These are the people which are responsible for saving my life and seeing me through all my horrors. This is my sister Chavilah with this young lady. At the time, her name is Tibel Kleinman. She's now called Tuchman. She worked for us in our photography business. And when she went to Russia, she took along those pictures. And thanks to her, I have all those pictures that she gave to me when she returned. This is the memorial for the 2,000 people that died on the outside of the river. My son, to the left, Ben Golub, went in 1988. He was the first Jewish man to come and step in that area after 50 years. Next to him is my cousin that lived in Russia at the time. His name is Samuel Tiktin. He subsequently moved to Israel with his family. Recently, the survivors of Ludwipol went to the place. And they erected a proper memorial to the people that died on that horrible day. Where you saw before my son and my cousin, next to this grave is now established a proper grave, an honorable burial for the deceased. On the new memorial, all the martyrs that were killed are listed by name. My family's name is line eight from the top-- Golub; Baruch, Gittel, Chava, and Esther. This is the River Sluch, which I had mentioned in my testimony. It divided our territory in half. When I spoke about moving from one side of the river to the other, this was the river that we had to cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about the picture, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son, Ben, and his lovely wife, Cynthia. She's holding my granddaughter, Alex. And he's holding my grandson, Philip. They are twins. This is the latest addition to our family. This is my son's newest born daughter. Her name is Jill. And she's 10 months old. This is my daughter, Elizabeth. Thanks to her, I agreed to give testimony. She urged me to tell my story. And I'm grateful to her. This is part of the memorial erected by our townspeople for the people that perished from Ludwipol.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do want to say what it says? No? OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Ruth. We are married 39 years. And she is the mother of my children and the grandmother of the grandchildren. I'd like to have her say few words, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: It has taken many years for Aharon to be able to talk about his experiences. I'm sure it's taken an emotional toll on him to do it. But I hope that this will serve a purpose and will help people not to forget what has happened and is such a way to learn and to grow.
